content.text:
{For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light.}{Matthew 11:30}
 {The method of least squares is the automobile of modern statistical analysis.}{— Stephen Stigler}

When $A\vect{x} = \vect{b}$ has no exact solution, we find the 
**least squares** solution that minimizes error.

content.title: Least Squares Solution
content.content:
A **least squares solution** of 
$A\vect{x} = \vect{b}$ is $\hat{\vect{x}}$ that minimizes 
$\norm{A\vect{x} - \vect{b}}$, the distance from $\vect{b}$ to $A\vect{x}$.

content.title: Normal Equations
content.content:
The least squares solutions of $A\vect{x} = \vect{b}$ are exactly 
the solutions of:
\[
A\transpose A\hat{\vect{x}} = A\transpose\vect{b}
\]
If columns of $A$ are independent, there is a unique solution:
\[
\hat{\vect{x}} = (A\transpose A)\inv A\transpose\vect{b}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Line of best fit
content.problem: Find the line $y = mx + c$ that best fits $(0, 1), (1, 1), (2, 3)$.
content.solution:
Set up $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, 
$\vect{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}$, 
$\vect{x} = \begin{bmatrix} m \\ c \end{bmatrix}$.

Solve normal equations to find $m = 1$, $c = \frac{1}{3}$.

marginNotes.text:
And we know that for those who love God 
all things work together for good.
